topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee was bitter, a familiar, unwelcome taste that mirrored the hollowness in my chest. I watched Liam laugh across the small cafe table, his easy camaraderie with everyone else a bright, infuriating spotlight. He was talking about a trip he’d taken, a memory I was certain I was central to. I traced the condensation on my glass, the chill seeping into my fingertips. When he finally turned to me, his smile was warm, but his eyes held a distant, unreadable quality. "That sounds amazing," he said, nodding vaguely. A small, sharp thing lodged itself beneath my ribs. I opened my mouth, intending to launch into the details of the adventure, the inside jokes that had defined those summers, but the words caught, snagged on some invisible wire. He just blinked, a flicker of confusion crossing his features before he smoothly pivoted to another topic. I just nodded, the movement stiff, and ordered another, pretending the silence wasn't screaming the truth: I was an echo in his past, a ghost he hadn't learned to recognize.

[story 2]

I kept catching my reflection in shop windows, searching for some sign that I was still tangible, still present in the landscape of his life. It was a desperate search. Every time I saw a familiar face, my stomach would clench, a tight, cold knot forming just below my sternum. Liam was across the street, engrossed in a book, his brow furrowed in concentration. I wanted to walk over, to simply say hello, to pull him into the shared history we’d built brick by shared secret. But the impulse died before it could manifest. Instead, I turned away quickly, the sound of my own hurried footsteps echoing too loudly on the pavement. A wave of profound disappointment washed over me, heavy and suffocating. I felt clumsy, suddenly oversized, as if my presence was an imposition, a misplaced artifact that didn't fit into his neat timeline. I stood there, breathing shallowly, wishing the concrete beneath my worn sneakers would swallow me whole.

[story 3]

The old photo album lay open on my lap, the glossy pages filled with grainy images of us—sun-drenched, reckless, utterly inseparable. I ran a thumb over the faded snapshot of us at the lake, the warmth of the paper doing nothing to thaw the icy feeling spreading through my limbs. I tried to conjure the feeling of that day, the sheer, uncomplicated joy we’d shared, but the memory felt thin, like tracing water on glass. I looked up, expecting to see Liam nearby, perhaps catching my eye and sharing a silent acknowledgment of that shared history. But he was engrossed in a conversation with a group of new acquaintances, his attention focused elsewhere. A strange, hollow ache settled in my throat. I closed the album with a soft, defeated snap, the sound sharp in the quiet room. I stood up, my movements slow and deliberate, gathering my things, a sense of utter irrelevance clinging to me like damp clothing.
